coredata.dc:title: Aconitic acid derived carbon dots: Conjugated interaction for the detection of folic acid and fluorescence targeted imaging of folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Jiali
Quan, Feifei
Zhao, Fengjiao
Wu, Chengxin
Wang, Zhaoyan
Zhou, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we utilized aconitic acid (AA) as the precursor for preparing carbon dots (AA-CDs) by a facile hydrothermal reaction, and the resulting AA-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield of 56.5% in aqueous solution. Preliminary experiments showed that the prepared AA-CDs without further surface passivation or modification could selectively interact with folic acid (FA), resulting in significant fluorescence quenching. Based on this, a sensitive method for FA analysis was developed with a detection limit of 40 nM (S/N = 3), which was then successfully applied for the detection of FA in food and pharmaceutical samples with the average recoveries of 95.0–105.3%. Furthermore, a weak fluorescent nanoprobe (FA-AA-CDs) was fabricated based on the conjugated interaction of FA and AA-CDs, and its feasibility was evaluated as a fluorescence turn-on nanoprobe for targeted imaging of cancer cells using Hela, SMMC-7721, and A549 cells as models that expressed different levels of folate receptors (FRs) on the cell surface. The results exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of these cells was in accordance with their FRs expression levels, testifying its potential of FA-AA-CDs for targeted imaging of cancer cells. Subsequently, the internalization mechanism of FA-AA-CDs into cells was elucidated via receptor-mediated endocytosis using control experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aconitic acid
Carbon dots
Folic acid
Folate receptor
Cancer cell
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence targeted imaging of cancer cells
Fluorescence sensing of FA using AA-CDs
Mechanism discussion for the interaction of FA and AA-CDs
Fluorescence targeted imaging of cancer cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectra of the prepared AA-CDs under various excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm (a), and the fluorescence responses of AA-CDs toward ambient environments, including solution pH (b), light illumination (c), and ion strengths (d). Insert in (a) shows the photographs of AA-CDs under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right, 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of AA-CDs (50 μg mL−1) upon gradual addition of FA from 0 to 400 μM, and (b) the dependence of fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) on FA concentration. Insert in (b) shows the plots of F0/F against FA concentration in the range of 1–100 μM.
Effect of potentially interfering substances to AA-CDs. The concentrations of AA-CDs and FA are respective 50 μg mL−1 and 300 μM, while other coexisting substances are all 1000 μM.

Fluorescence microscopy images of Hela (FR++), SMMC-7721 (FR+), and A549 (FR−) cells incubated with AA-CDs (A, E, I) and FA-AA-CDs (C, G, K) for 2 h, and their corresponding bright-field images (B, F, J; D, H, L).

Fluorescence microscopy images of Hela cells incubated with FA-AA-CDs for 2 h (A), pretreated with excess of FA for FRs saturation and then incubated with FA-AA-CDs for 2 h (C), and their corresponding bright-field images (B; D).
Detection of FA in food and pharmaceutical samples using AA-CDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The marked content is 400 μg per tablet.

head.ce:title: Aconitic acid derived carbon dots: Conjugated interaction for the detection of folic acid and fluorescence targeted imaging of folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we utilized aconitic acid (AA) as the precursor for preparing carbon dots (AA-CDs) by a facile hydrothermal reaction, and the resulting AA-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield of 56.5% in aqueous solution. Preliminary experiments showed that the prepared AA-CDs without further surface passivation or modification could selectively interact with folic acid (FA), resulting in significant fluorescence quenching. Based on this, a sensitive method for FA analysis was developed with a detection limit of 40 nM (S/N = 3), which was then successfully applied for the detection of FA in food and pharmaceutical samples with the average recoveries of 95.0–105.3%. Furthermore, a weak fluorescent nanoprobe (FA-AA-CDs) was fabricated based on the conjugated interaction of FA and AA-CDs, and its feasibility was evaluated as a fluorescence turn-on nanoprobe for targeted imaging of cancer cells using Hela, SMMC-7721, and A549 cells as models that expressed different levels of folate receptors (FRs) on the cell surface. The results exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of these cells was in accordance with their FRs expression levels, testifying its potential of FA-AA-CDs for targeted imaging of cancer cells. Subsequently, the internalization mechanism of FA-AA-CDs into cells was elucidated via receptor-mediated endocytosis using control experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have attracted considerable attention due to their distinctive optical properties [1–3]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, CDs have some unique advantages such as stable physical and chemical properties, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and low production cost, easy surface functionalization, which enable them to show great potential in analysis, sensing, and bioimaging [4–7]. The fluorescence properties of CDs are closely related to their surface structures, and CDs can generally interact with analytes to change the recombination efficiency of the electron/hole pair, resulting in either enhancement or quenching in the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs for subsequent qualitative or quantitative analysis. CDs usually contain some coordination groups such as carboxyl, amino, and/or mercapto groups, etc., and accordingly they are often applied as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of metal ions [8,9]. Profited from the developments of CDs’ preparation and functionalization, CDs are increasingly used to determine life-related substances in recent years [10,11]. However, the relevant research has just emerged on the horizon, and as a consequence, preparing new CDs, designing novel analytical strategies, and exploring their applications in bio-analytical field are still the current research focus.

Folic acid (FA), a water-soluble vitamin, is involved in a variety of physiological processes as substrate and coenzyme [12]. FA deficiency or excessive intake will lead to the occurrence of some diseases, and as is well known, adequate intake of FA can prevent neural tube developmental defects during pregnancy [13]. Most mammals, including humans, cannot synthesize FA by themselves and thus must obtain it from exogenous sources via intestinal absorption. Therefore, it is essential for the development of reliable, selective, and sensitive methods for the detection of FA in food and pharmaceutical samples [14,15]. Up to now, the researchers have developed multitudinous methods for the determination of FA, including microbiological method [16], immunoassay [17,18], luminescence analysis [19,20], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [21,22], chromatographic techniques [23–25], and electrochemical determination [26]. However, most of these methods have some limitations, such as the requirement of expensive equipment, long operation times, poor selectivity, low sensitivity, and so on. Among these, fluorescence-based methods possess excellent selectivity and sensitivity, and their core is to design fluorescent probes [27–29]. Hence, the development of low toxicity and low cost fluorescent probes is of great significance for FA analysis.

Folate receptors (FRs), high-affinity folate-binding proteins (Kd ∼ 10−10 M), are known to be overexpressed on the membranes of many cancer cells, but minimally expressed on normal cells [30,31]. Thus, various FA-functionalized nanoprobes were developed based on the specific affinity of FRs and FA for fluorescence targeted imaging of cancer cells [32–37]. Considering its excellent fluorescence property and biocompatibility, CDs would be a better alternative as a nanoprobe for cellular imaging. Recently, Bhunia et al. reported the construction of CDs derived from FA for imaging cancer cells [38]. Also, some researchers utilized different passivating agents to modify CDs, and then fabricated FA-functionalized fluorescent nanoprobes through the conjugated interaction between FA and passivated CDs for targeted imaging of cancer cells [39–42]. Nevertheless, the passivation is tedious and time-consuming, and the passivating agents would affect the affinity of FA to FRs, thereby disturbing subsequent targeted imaging.

In our previous work, aconitic acid (AA) has been testified to be an ideal precursor to produce CDs with excellent fluorescence properties [43]. Herein, the absolute quantum yield of AA derived carbon dots (AA-CDs) was further improved to 56.5% by a facile hydrothermal reaction using ethylenediamine as co-doping reagent. More importantly, without further surface passivation or modification, the prepared AA-CDs could interact with FA, leading to significant quenching in its intrinsic fluorescence. Based on these, the objective of this work is to develop a simple and sensitive sensor using AA-CDs for FA analysis in food and pharmaceutical samples. Subsequently, FA conjugated AA-CDs (FA-AA-CDs) were expected as a weak fluorescence nanoprobe to be readily recognized by FRs on the cell surface, as a consequence, the AA-CDs were released into the cells and their fluorescence was recovered. This is a dynamic process that makes the FA-AA-CDs be beneficial for targeted imaging of FR overexpressed cancer cells or distinguishing the cells with different FRs expression levels.

Aconitic acid (AA, analytical reagent grade) was obtained from Alfa Aesar Chemistry (Tianjin, China). 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) was of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Baishi Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Folic Acid (FA) was received from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, and citric acid were all purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. Amino acids including histidine (His), lysine (Lys), threonine (Thr), valine (Val), tryptophan (Try), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), phenylalanine (Phe), and methionine (Met) were all of analytical regent grade and received from Xi’an Zhoudingguo Biotech (Xi’an, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.5 MΩ cm was used throughout all the experiments. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM) was prepared from 8.00 g NaCl, 0.20 g KCl, 1.42 g Na2HPO4, and 0.28 g KH2PO4 in 1 L ultrapure water and adjusted to a desired pH for use.

The AA-CDs were synthesized by a typical hydrothermal method. Briefly, AA (0.0871 g) and EDA (100.4 μL) were dissolved in 5.0 mL ultrapure water. Then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 150 °C for 5 h until the color of the mixture solution turned to brownish-yellow, suggesting the formation of AA-CDs. The brownish-yellow solution was then desiccated in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 8 h to obtain the AA-CDs powders. The powders, which can be easily dispersed into water to form stable and uniform solution, were stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

All fluorescence spectra were performed on Perking FL-4500 spectrofluorometer equipped with a quartz cell (1 × l cm). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were surveyed on a UV 2800SPC spectrophotometer (Sunny Hengping Scientific Instrument, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 1 quartz cell. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of AA-CDs was determined using an FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.) and the integrating sphere accessory. For the determination of absolute QY, only dilute solutions and the corresponding solvent at the excitation wavelength (λex = 360 nm) were used. The fluorescence decay lifetime of AA-CDs and FA-AA-CDs was also measured on the FLS920 spectrometer at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The zeta potentials of AA-CDs and FA in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) were performed on Malvern Zetasizer 3000HSa (He-Ne laser, λ = 632.8 nm).

For the detection of FA, 50 μL of AA-CDs (4 mg mL−1) was diluted with PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) to final concentration of 50 μg mL−1, followed by addition of FA with different concentrations. After ultrasonic mixing and incubation for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of AA-CDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the fluorescence intensities were monitored at an emission wavelength of 450 nm. The fluorescence responses of AA-CDs to common metal ions, organics, and essential amino acids were also studied using the same procedure.

Food samples including oatmeal, liquid milk and fruit juice were all purchased from a local supermarket, and FA tablets as pharmaceutical samples were obtained from a local pharmacy. Typical sample preparations were as follows: one of three tablets grounded into powder was fully dissolved into 5.0 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution; an accurately weighed amount of oatmeal (0.3 g) was dispersed in 5.0 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution; liquid milk (1.0 g) was dispersed in 5.0 mL of 0.05 M acetic acid solution, and then the mixtures were centrifuged for 20 min at 12,000 rpm, respectively. All supernatants were collected separately, in which 0.3 mL of 0.1 M AgNO3 solution and 0.3 mL of 0.1 M BaCl2 solution were added in turn, and the mixtures were shaken well. Another 10 min centrifugation later, the pH values of all supernatants were adjusted to 12.0 with NaOH solution. After that, the mixtures of the above three pretreated samples and commercial fruit juice were all centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm and filtered using 0.22 μm micropore cellulose acetate membrane, and the filtrates were diluted with PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence targeted imaging of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: For the construction of FA-AA-CDs as a weak fluorescence nanoprobe, FA (50 μL, 24 mM) was added into 4.0 mL of the AA-CDs (150 μg mL−1) in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4), and then the FA-AA-CDs solution was ultrasonically mixed fully (5 min) and kept in dark for further experiments. Hela, SMMC-7721 and A549 cells, those possessed different expression levels of FRs, were used as models for cell imaging. Hela and A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco minimum essential medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), while SMMC-7721 cells were cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 (RPMI-1640) supplemented of 5% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% streptomycin and penicillin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 48 h. After that, the cells were seeded in 12-well plates (1 mL per well) and cultured for 24 h, and then incubated for another 2 h after the addition of 50 μL AA-CDs or FA-AA-CDs. Moreover, for further proving the receptor-mediated endocytosis of FA-AA-CDs nanoprobes into cells, the control experiments were implemented where the Hela cells were pre-incubated with excess of FA for 2 h prior to FA-AA-CDs incubation. The fluorescence images were taken on an Olympus fluorescence microscope (Japan) at the excitation wavelength of 340–390 nm after fully washing cells with PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) and fixing the cells on the slide.

Aconitic acid has been demonstrated to be an ideal precursor for the preparation of CDs because of its unsaturated bond in the molecular structure. Briefly, the prepared AA-CDs were mono-dispersed and uniform with an average size of 1.6 nm (Fig. S1). Attached onto their surface, a variety of hydrophilic functional groups including amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties were present (Figs. S2 and S3). Accordingly, the prepared AA-CDs were water-soluble, and its aqueous solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm, inset in Fig. 1 a). The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1a) exhibited two absorption bands at about 245 and 350 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of AA-CDs, respectively [44]. With continuously increasing the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, no obvious wavelength shift was observed in the fluorescence emission spectra of AA-CDs, implying an excitation-independent fluorescent behavior (Fig. 1a). The fluorescence spectra showed that the optimum emission of AA-CDs was located at 450 nm when excited at 360 nm. In addition, the absolute quantum yield of AA-CDs was improved from 45.1% to 56.5% by using hydrothermal instead of microwave-assisted carbonization method [43], which provided a strong guarantee for the sensitivity of subsequent analytical applications using AA-CDs as fluorescent nanoprobes. Further, the fluorescence stability of AA-CDs was investigated against ambient environments including solution pH (Fig. 1b), UV exposure (Fig. 1c), and ionic strengths (Fig. 1d). The obtained AA-CDs gave maximal and comparatively stable fluorescence emission at pH 4.0–11.0 in PBS solution (10 mM), while the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increase of pH from 1.5 to 4.0 and decreased with the increase of pH from 11.0 to 12.0; this behavior may be closely related to the surface group of AA-CDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of AA-CDs in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) showed no distinct decrease after UV illumination for 5 h (λex = 360 nm). And also, ionic strengths had little influence on the fluorescence stability of AA-CDs, even under the extreme condition of 4 M NaCl solution. Such excellent fluorescence properties enable the prepared AA-CDs to be highly suitable for biosystem sensing and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of FA using AA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary experiments showed that without further surface passivation or modification, the intrinsic fluorescence of AA-CDs could be gradually quenched by incremental introduction of FA. For the construction of a robust and sensitive sensor, some parameters including AA-CDs concentration, solution pH, and incubating time were systematically investigated. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) of FA to AA-CDs was studied as a dependent variable versus the concentrations of AA-CDs in the range of 10–100 μg mL−1 (Fig. S4), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm in the absence and presence of FA, respectively. The results indicated that the F0/F increased with increasing AA-CDs concentration in PBS solution, but further increase beyond 50 μg mL−1 was followed by a slight increase of F0/F. So the AA-CDs concentration of 50 μg mL−1 was selected for further study. Afterwards, the solution pH was investigated in the range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. S5), and actually, the F0/F was not almost affected by solution pH in the range of 4.0–9.0. Considering the possibility of subsequent applications in biosystem, the physiological pH of 7.4 was chosen for further study. Furthermore, the incubating time (0–30 min) was investigated on the quenching efficiency, and beyond 1 min, the F0/F hardly changed, suggesting faster kinetics. In conclusion, all measurements were performed after incubation for 1 min in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) with AA-CDs concentration of 50 μg mL−1.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of AA-CDs against FA concentration was quantitatively investigated in detail (Fig. 2 ), and the results indicated that a good linear relationship is attainable in the range of 1–100 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. The detection limit of FA was 40 nM by calculation from S/N = 3, indicating an adequate sensitivity for the analysis of FA in real samples. Except for the sensitivity, the selectivity is general another important factor for a sensor. The fluorescence responses of AA-CDs to other potentially coexisting substances were studied including common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+), amino acids (His, Lys, Thr, Val, Try, Leu, Ile, Phe, Met), and organics (glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, citric acid). As shown in Fig. 3 , the intrinsic fluorescence of AA-CDs exhibited no significant change (less than 10%) by the introduction of coexisting substances with higher concentration (1000 μM). The outstanding sensitivity and selectivity demonstrated the feasibility of AA-CDs as fluorescent nanoprobe for the analysis of FA. And then, the practicability was evaluated using the prepared AA-CDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe for FA analysis in complex matrix, including food (oatmeal, liquid milk, and fruit juice) and pharmaceutical (FA tablets) samples. As summarized in Table 1 , the average recoveries calculated from three spiked levels were in the range of 95.0–105.3%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were less than 6.5%, demonstrating its feasibility. Benefit from high quantum yield and unique surface properties, the AA-CDs based sensor is testified to be robust for FA analysis in complex food and pharmaceutical samples. One can speculate that assisting with sample enrichment techniques [45], the sensitivity of this sensor can be further improved for FA analysis in more complicated samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism discussion for the interaction of FA and AA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of FA with AA-CDs leads to its fluorescence quenching, and so further exploring the interaction mechanism is significant for the construction of weak fluorescent nanoprobes. Firstly, we compared the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA with the excitation and emission spectrum of AA-CDs, as shown in Fig. S6. The molecular absorption of FA in the ultraviolet region, centered at 280 and 350 nm, had almost no overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of AA-CDs (450 nm), excluding the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from AA-CDs to FA [46]. The inner filter effect (IFE) is another important non-irradiation energy conversion process, especially when the absorption spectrum of absorber possesses an overlap region with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of fluorescer. For a typical IFE process, the addition of absorber does not change the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescer [47]. It is observed that FA had a weak absorption at 350 nm that overlapped with the excitation of AA-CDs (Fig. S6). After the addition of FA, the absorption of mixed system was almost unchanged (Fig. S7), while the fluorescence lifetime of FA-AA-CDs changed significantly (Fig. S8). Then, the IFE could be excluded. Generally, the fluorescence quenching is divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. To elucidate the underlying mechanism of fluorescence quenching of AA-CDs by FA, the fluorescence spectrum and quenching efficiency of AA-CDs in the presence of FA at various concentrations was studied. The fluorescence intensity of AA-CDs in PBS solution at 450 nm gradually decreased following addition of FA, while the fluorescence wavelength showed no appreciable changes (Fig. 2a). Plotting of F0/F versus the concentration of FA yielded an upward curve (Fig. 2b), suggesting that both dynamic quenching and static quenching are possible [48]. Subsequently, a careful comparison between the UV–vis absorption bands of AA-CDs and FA was performed (Fig. S7). The results indicated that with the addition of FA into the AA-CDs solution, no new UV–vis absorption bands were observable, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching between the AA-CDs and FA was not induced by the change in excitation wavelength. Accordingly, the static quenching mechanism on the basis of the formation of the grounded-state complex could be excluded [49]. Further experimental results revealed that when 30 and 300 μM FA was introduced into the AA-CDs solution, the fluorescence lifetime of AA-CDs was decreased from 14.20 to 13.49 and 11.26 ns (Fig. S8), evidencing that it is not the static quenching for AA-CDs by FA. In addition, the zeta potentials of AA-CDs and FA in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) were measured to be respective 0.67 and −42.00 mV, suggesting that FA was easily adsorbed onto the surface of AA-CDs by electrostatic interaction and then induced electron transfer [40]. Based on above discussions, the AA-CDs can interact with FA via electrostatic interaction, by which an electron-transfer process will occur between them, resulting in dynamic quenching in the fluorescence of AA-CDs [50]. Then, one can speculate that in the presence of a stronger receptor, the FA will be released from the surface of FA-AA-CDs to recover the intrinsic fluorescence of AA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence targeted imaging of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the prepared AA-CDs were evaluated its cytotoxicity with MTT assays using Hela cells as a model (Fig. S9). The results showed that the cell viability and morphology was not significantly affected after incubation with AA-CDs for 24 h, indicating excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Generally, folate receptors are well known to be overexpressed by various cancer cells but rarely expressed in normal cells. So it can be speculated that, based on the specific affinity between FA and FRs, FA conjugated AA-CDs are more readily entered cancer cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis, and simultaneously, FA is released from the surface of AA-CDs, by which the intrinsic fluorescence of entered AA-CDs will be recovered. In this study, three types of cancer cells with different FRs expression levels were selected as models to evaluate the feasibility of FA-AA-CDs as nanoprobes for turn-on fluorescence targeted imaging of FRs overexpressed cancer cells, including Hela cells (a human cervical cancer cell with overexpression of FRs, named FR++), SMMC-7721 cells (a human hepatoma carcinoma cell with high-expression of FRs, named FR+), and A549 cells (adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cells with negative expression of FRs, named FR−) [35]. The results showed that the fluorescence intensities of three cells were different after incubated with FA-AA-CDs under the same conditions, Hela cells were the brightest, SMMC-7721 cells were the second, and A549 cells were the weakest; while the fluorescence intensities of three cells were comparable following incubation with AA-CDs as a control (Fig. 4 ). The fluorescence intensities of FA-AA-CDs stained cancer cells were consistent with their expression levels of FRs, indicating that the prepared FA-AA-CDs could be used as fluorescent nanoprobes to distinguish FR positive-expressed cancer cells from negative-expressed normal cells.

During the experiments, it was observed that FA-AA-CDs incubated Hela cells were general brighter than those incubated with AA-CDs under the same conditions, indicating that AA-CDs surface-coupled FA makes the CDs more beneficial to enter cells. Further control experiments were carried out to validate the hypothesis using Hela cells as a model. Namely, the Hela cells were firstly incubated with excess FA for 2 h to saturate the FRs on their surface, and then incubated with FA-AA-CDs for another 2 h. Compared with the Hela cells without FA saturation, the fluorescence intensity of FA saturated Hela cells was significantly attenuated (Fig. 5 ), demonstrating the internalization of FA-AA-CDs nanoprobes into cancer cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis. It is well known that targeted imaging of cancer cells is important for advancing cancer research. Recently, FA derived CDs [38] and FA covalently modified CDs [51] had been developed for targeted imaging of cancer cells, in which their fluorescence did not change before and after cell staining, and also these probes had a tendency to be anchored to the cytomembrane. By using different passivating agents to modify CDs, a series of FA conjugated CDs were fabricated for targeted imaging of cancer cells [39–42], while the passivation process was tedious and time-consuming, and the passivating agents could bring some unexpected side effects for subsequent targeted imaging. Given the unique surface properties, we constructed here a weak fluorescence nanoprobe of FA-AA-CDs via the conjugated interaction of FA and AA-CDs without surface passivation or modification. The obtained FA-AA-CDs can be easily recognized by FRs on the cell surface by which the AA-CDs were released into the cells and meanwhile their strong fluorescence was recovered. Such a dynamic process enables the prepared FA-AA-CDs to take significant advantages in targeted imaging of FR overexpressed cancer cells.

In this study, aconitic acid was applied as a carbon precursor for preparing fluorescent carbon dots by a facile hydrothermal method using ethylenediamine as the co-doping reagent. The AA-CDs were water-soluble and its aqueous solution exhibited excitation-independent, bright blue fluorescence with an absolute quantum yield of 56.5%. Without further surface passivation or modification, the intrinsic fluorescence of AA-CDs could be significantly quenched by FA through their conjugated interaction, and based on this, a fluorescent sensor was fabricated for the sensitive and selective detection of FA with a detection limit of 40 nM. The obtained AA-CDs were successfully applied for the detection of FA in food and pharmaceutical samples with the average recoveries in the range of 95.0–105.3% and the relative standard deviation of less than 6.5%, testifying good accuracy and precision. Furthermore, a weak fluorescent nanoprobe (FA-AA-CDs) was fabricated based on the conjugated interaction of FA and AA-CDs, and the prepared FA-AA-CDs could enter cells by receptor-mediated endocytosis. In this process, the FRs on cell surface could competitively bind to FA, leading the entered AA-CDs to recover its intrinsic fluorescence. Depending on the difference of FRs on cell surface, FA-AA-CDs could distinguish Hela, SMMC-7721, and A549 cells with different FRs expression levels, demonstrating its feasibility for turn-on fluorescence targeted imaging of FRs overexpressed cancer cells. Given the advantages of AA-CDs, the weak fluorescent nanoprobe FA-AA-CDs is expected to show great potential as a new tool for the diagnosis, therapy, and prognosis of cancer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675069, 21527808, and 21405067) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2017-111).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.227.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Carbon dots-emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots
Sensing applications of luminescent carbon based dots
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
Carbon quantum dot-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection
Recent advances in bioapplications of C-dots
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Folic acid: nutritional biochemistry, molecular biology, and role in disease processes
The analysis of folate and its metabolic precursors in biological samples
Determination of folate concentrations in diverse potato germplasm using a trienzyme extraction and a microbiological assay
Disposable microfluidic ELISA for the rapid determination of folic acid content in food products
Immunoassay for folic acid detection in vitamin-fortified milk based on electrochemical magneto sensors
Detection of ascorbic acid and folic acid based on water-soluble CuInS2 quantum dots
Fluorescence probe based on an amino-functionalized fluorescent magnetic nanocomposite for detection of folic acid in serum
A binary functional substrate for enrichment and ultrasensitive SERS spectroscopic detection of folic acid using graphene oxide/Ag nanoparticle hybrids
SERS encoded nanoparticle heterodimers for the ultrasensitive detection of folic acid
Determination of folic acid by CE in various cultivated variety of lentils
A validated ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method for the selective analysis of free and total folate in plasma and red blood cells
High performance liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry for profiling and quantitative analysis of folate monoglutamates in tomato
Electrochemical determination of folic acid: a short review
BSA-modified gold nanoclusters for sensing of folic acid
Polyethylenimine-capped silver nanoclusters as a fluorescence probe for highly sensitive detection of folic acid through a two-step electron-transfer process
PVA-based nanobiosensor for ultrasensitive detection of folic acid by fluorescence quenching
Structural basis for molecular recognition of folic acid by folate receptors
Folate binding protein-outlook for drug delivery applications
Folate-receptor-mediated delivery of InP quantum dots for bioimaging using confocal and two-photon microscopy
A folate receptor-targeting nanoparticle minimizes drug resistance in a human cancer model
Distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cells from folate-receptor-negative cells using a fluorescence off-on nanoprobe
Self-assembly of folate onto polyethyleneimine-coated CdS/ZnS quantum dots for targeted turn-on fluorescence imaging of folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells
Fabrication of folic acid-sensitive gold nanoclusters for turn-on fluorescent imaging of overexpression of folate receptor in tumor cells
Folic acid-conjugated fluorescent polymer for up-regulation folate receptor expression study via targeted imaging of tumor cells
Imaging cancer cells expressing the folate receptor with carbon dots produced from folic acid
Fluorescent carbon nanodots conjugated with folic acid for distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
Non-covalent decoration of carbon dots with folic acid via a polymer-assisted strategy for fast and targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging
PEI-folic acid modified carbon nanodots for cancer cell-targeted delivery and two-photon excitation imaging
Aconitic acid derived carbon dots as recyclable on-off-onfluorescent nanoprobes for sensitive detection of mercury (II) ions, cysteine and cellular imaging
On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence probe combined with magnetic solid-phase extraction purification for analysis of folic acid in human serum
Photoinduced electron transfer of DNA/Ag nanoclusters modulated by G-quadruplex/hemin complex for the construction of versatile biosensors
Inner filter effect-based fluorescent sensing systems: a review
Electron transfer quenching by nitroxide radicals of the fluorescence of carbon dots
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
Folic acid-conjugated carbon dots as green fluorescent probes based on cellular targeting imaging for recognizing cancer cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiali Qian received her M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University in 2017. Her research was on the preparation and analytical application of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials.

Feifei Quan is a graduate student at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. She received her B.S. degree from Northwest Normal University in 2015. She is currently engaged in developing advanced functional materials for analytical chemistry.

Fengjiao Zhao received her M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2016 at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. Her research interests focus on preparation and analytical applications of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials.

Chengxin Wu is working for his M.S. degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. He received his B.S. degree from Lanzhou University in 2016. He is focused on developing advanced functional materials for analytical chemistry.

Zhaoyan Wang is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, PR China. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Lanzhou University. Her current research interests include pharmaceutical analysis with microchip electrophoresis/capillary electrophoresis, synthesis of new fluorescent materials and their application.

Lei Zhou is currently an associate professor in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Lanzhou University. He received his Ph.D. degree in 2008 from Lanzhou University. From 2013 to 2014, he worked as a postdoctoral research associate in Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at the University of Oklahoma. His main research interests focus on advanced functional materials for analytical chemistry, and capillary electrophoresis and microfluidic analysis.
